questions.question: Explain the three essential requirements that a solution to the Critical Section problem must satisfy.
keyPoints: Mutual Exclusion: Only one process can be in its critical section at any time
Progress: When no process is in critical section and processes wish to enter, only those in remainder section can participate in decision
Bounded Waiting: After a process requests entry, there is a limit on how many other processes can enter before this process gets access

questions.question: What is a Race Condition? How does it relate to the Critical Section problem?
keyPoints: Race Condition: When the final outcome depends on the relative timing of execution of multiple processes
In Critical Section context: Multiple processes accessing/modifying shared data concurrently leads to inconsistent results
The Critical Section problem is designed to prevent race conditions through proper synchronization

questions.question: Describe the Test-and-Set instruction and how it is used to implement mutual exclusion.
keyPoints: Test-and-Set is a hardware atomic instruction that reads and modifies a variable in one indivisible operation
It returns the old value while setting the variable to true atomically
Used in a while loop: while (TestAndSet(&lock)) { } - if lock was false, returns false and enters critical section
Provides mutual exclusion but may cause busy waiting

questions.question: What is Peterson's Solution for the Critical Section problem? Explain the shared variables used.
keyPoints: Peterson's Solution is a software-based algorithm for two processes
Uses two shared variables: flag[2] boolean array and turn integer variable
flag[i]=true indicates process i wants to enter critical section
turn variable indicates whose turn it is
Both processes execute entry code checking flag of other process and turn value

questions.question: Explain the difference between busy waiting (spinlock) and blocking synchronization mechanisms.
keyPoints: Busy Waiting: Process continuously checks a condition in a loop, wasting CPU cycles
Blocking: Process is put to sleep by OS and woken up when resource is available
Spinlocks are efficient for short critical sections on multi-processor systems
Blocking is better for long critical sections to avoid CPU waste

questions.question: What are the four sections of code in the structure of a process solution for the Critical Section problem?
keyPoints: Entry Section: Code that requests permission and acquires necessary synchronization
Critical Section: Code that accesses shared resources requiring mutual exclusion
Exit Section: Code that releases locks and notifies other processes
Remainder Section: Remaining code that does not require mutual exclusion

questions.question: Why is atomicity important in solving the Critical Section problem?
keyPoints: Atomicity ensures operations execute as a single indivisible unit
Without atomicity, read-modify-write operations can be interleaved causing race conditions
Hardware instructions like Test-and-Set provide atomicity
Software solutions rely on atomic execution of certain operations

questions.question: Explain Peterson's algorithm for two processes in detail. Does it satisfy all three requirements of the Critical Section problem? Justify your answer.
keyPoints: Algorithm uses flag[2] and turn variables
Process i sets flag[i]=true and turn=i before entry
Entry code: while (flag[j] && turn==j) { }
Exit code sets flag[i]=false
Analysis: Mutual Exclusion - satisfied because while loop blocks if other wants entry and it's their turn
Progress - satisfied as processes not in critical section can set flags
Bounded Waiting - satisfied as turn gives fair chance, but modern hardware memory models may cause issues

questions.question: Discuss the hardware approach to solving the Critical Section problem. Explain how Test-and-Set and Swap instructions provide mutual exclusion.
keyPoints: Hardware solutions use atomic hardware instructions
Test-and-Set: Atomically reads, stores old value, and sets to true - implemented in while loop for lock
Swap: Atomically exchanges values of two variables - used to swap local 'key' with shared 'lock'
Both provide mutual exclusion but use busy waiting
Advantages: applicable to any number of processes, simple to implement
Disadvantages: busy waiting wastes CPU, may cause starvation, priority inversion problems

questions.question: What are the limitations of software-only solutions like Peterson's algorithm? Discuss memory reordering issues and modern considerations.
keyPoints: Peterson's algorithm only works for two processes
Assumes sequential memory consistency which may not hold on modern processors
Memory reordering: compilers and CPUs may reorder memory operations for optimization
On multi-core systems, writes by one process may not be immediately visible to another
Memory barriers (fences) may be needed to enforce ordering
Modern hardware with weak memory models can violate assumptions of classical solutions

questions.question: Compare and contrast mutual exclusion, progress, and bounded waiting requirements. Why are all three necessary for a correct solution?
keyPoints: Mutual Exclusion: Prevents simultaneous access - most fundamental requirement
Progress: Ensures decision-making when CS is free - prevents indefinite postponement
Bounded Waiting: Ensures fairness - prevents starvation

All three needed: Mutual exclusion alone not enough (processes could never enter), Progress alone not enough (mutual exclusion not ensured), Bounded waiting alone not enough (processes could be blocked indefinitely)
Trade-offs: Some solutions may optimize for one at expense of others
